message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

LENS: implementation reality. Grep/read the RBK bash under Tools/rbk/ (e.g. rbgg_Governor.sh, rbgp_Payor.sh, rba_Auth.sh, rbgv_AccessProbe.sh, rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh, rbcc_Constants.sh, rba_cli.sh). Verify the design's implicit assumptions hold in code: how the token accessor is keyed today, where RBRA file paths are consumed (the "~30 sites" claim), how SA names are composed (director-<identity>), how mantle/invest/divest are structured. Flag any design claim the code contradicts, and any migration hazard the design omits (e.g. existing callers, constants, derived paths).

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241
(Bash completed with no output)

21	set -euo pipefail
22	
23	# Multiple inclusion detection
24	test -z "${ZRBCC_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"

28	# so its own directory IS the kit dir. No launcher environment dependency;
29	# available the instant this file is sourced (BCG kit-self-location pattern,
30	# canon: rbtd/rbte_cli.sh).

34	# (write-on-change), rbq's gates verify them. Absolute, composed from the
35	# readonly kit dir (source-time, no kindle dependency).

37	readonly RBCC_tabtarget_context_file="${RBCC_KIT_DIR}/claude-rbk-tabtarget-context.md"
38	
39	# ── Moorings inventory constants ──────────────────────────────────────────
40	# Moorings-relative path values. Every consumer reads these names directly
41	# (the transitional RBBC_* aliases were retired by the literal-sweep pace).
42	# Source-time literals, no kindle dependency.
43	RBCC_moorings_dir="rbmm_moorings"
44	RBCC_launchers_subdir="rbml_launchers"
45	RBCC_users_subdir="rbmu_users"
46	RBCC_nodes_subdir="rbmn_nodes"
47	RBCC_vessels_subdir="rbmv_vessels"
48	RBCC_rbrr_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrr.env"
49	RBCC_rbrp_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrp.env"
50	RBCC_rbrm_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/rbrm.env"
51	RBCC_rbrd_basename="rbrd.env"
52	RBCC_rbrd_file="${RBCC_moorings_dir}/${RBCC_rbrd_basename}"
53	
54	# Literal constants (pure string literals, no variable expansion — available at source time)
55	RBCC_rbrs_file="../station-files/rbrs.env"
56	RBCC_rbrn_file="rbrn.env"
57	RBCC_rbra_file="rbra.env"
58	RBCC_rbro_file="rbro.env"
59	# Auth-role enum (rbnae_): the RBRA_ROLE value — minted sprue, RBRA_ROLE domain
60	# (3 members). Written into credential files, validated, carried as the auth
61	# folio, swizzle-checked against BUZ_FOLIO. Distinct axis from RBCC_account_*
62	# below: that one is the bare composition label.
63	RBCC_role_governor="rbnae_governor"
64	RBCC_role_retriever="rbnae_retriever"
65	RBCC_role_director="rbnae_director"
66	
67	# Account composition labels — bare fragments that compose GCP SA account-ids/
68	# emails AND local secret-directory names (6 members, superset of the 3 roles
69	# plus payor/assay/mason which are NEVER RBRA_ROLE values). These stay bare:
70	# a derived resource-name string in cloud/filesystem space, structurally like
71	# the SA email and the -tether/-airgap pool suffixes the heat keeps bare.
72	RBCC_account_governor="governor"
73	RBCC_account_retriever="retriever"
74	RBCC_account_director="director"
75	RBCC_account_payor="payor"
76	RBCC_account_assay="assay"
77	RBCC_account_mason="mason"
78	RBCC_onboarding_nameplate="tadmor"
79	
80	# Operation-verb tinder — the canonical bash home for RBK operation verbs.
81	# Members are bare verb tokens; the group carries one author here so it is
82	# projectable under the single-canonical-author rule. Two surfaces:
83	#
84	#   SA-management (divest/invest/roster) — composed into the fact-extension
85	#   constants below; consumed by the governor/director account surface.
86	RBCC_verb_divest="divest"
87	RBCC_verb_invest="invest"
88	RBCC_verb_roster="roster"
89	#
90	#   Image/build lifecycle (enshrine/inscribe/kludge/ordain/yoke) — name the
91	#   registry and build operations. Previously implicit in command-function
92	#   names (rbfl_inscribe, rbfd_ordain, …) and tabtarget descriptions; homed
93	#   here so the group has a single owner rather than being reconstructed by
94	#   grep across rbfd_/rbfl_/rbfk_/rbob_.
95	RBCC_verb_enshrine="enshrine"
96	RBCC_verb_inscribe="inscribe"
97	RBCC_verb_kludge="kludge"
98	RBCC_verb_ordain="ordain"
99	RBCC_verb_yoke="yoke"
100	
101	# Fact-file extension tinder — multi-fact registry for buf_write_fact_multi.
102	# Producers emit "<basename>.<extension>" via filesystem-as-data-bus pattern;
103	# consumers walk fact files in BURD_OUTPUT_DIR / BURD_TEMP_DIR keyed on extension.
104	# Roster extensions composed from earlier tinder (BCG tinder-on-tinder).
105	RBCC_fact_ext_depot="depot"
106	RBCC_fact_ext_depot_project="depot-project"
107	RBCC_fact_ext_roster_retriever="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_account_retriever}"
108	RBCC_fact_ext_roster_director="${RBCC_verb_roster}-${RBCC_account_director}"
109	RBCC_fact_ext_audit_hallmark="audit-hallmark"
110	RBCC_fact_ext_lode="lode"
111	
112	# Container-role tinder — the canonical bash home for the crucible's container
113	# roles. Bare role tokens; the crucible is sentry + pentacle + bottle and every
114	# container name / compose service derives from these. Distinct from the
115	# credential auth-role axis above, which itself splits in two: RBCC_role_*
116	# (minted enum, the RBRA_ROLE value) and RBCC_account_* (bare composition label
117	# for SA names + secret dirs). None of these words are reused across families,
118	# keeping each token monosemous.
119	RBCC_container_bottle="bottle"
120	RBCC_container_pentacle="pentacle"
121	RBCC_container_sentry="sentry"
122	
123	######################################################################
124	# Rust const projection (rbcc single-homed set → RBTDGC_)
125	
126	# rbcc_emit_consts() - Emit the RBCC-owned co-maintained constants as Rust
127	# string consts to stdout, one `pub const` line per name/value pair via the
128	# shared buz_emit_const primitive (BUK must be kindled). The single-homed set:
129	# moorings/vessels dirs, account labels, .env filenames, operation verbs, and
130	# container roles. Each Rust const is
131	# RBTDGC_ + the RBCC stem (RBCC_ prefix stripped) uppercased; the value is
132	# carried verbatim. Bash stays mixed-case (RBCC_moorings_dir); the generated
133	# Rust is SCREAMING (RBTDGC_MOORINGS_DIR) per Rust convention — that casing is
134	# the sole transform, applied mechanically, so there is no per-entry mapping
135	# and no drift. rbtd's lib.rs paths, the manifest account-label mirror, and the
136	# rbtdrk/rbtdrp .env consts all source these instead of hand-copying.
137	rbcc_emit_consts() {
138	  printf '%s\n' "// RBCC constants (rbcc_Constants.sh single-homed set)"
139	
140	  local z_name=""
141	  local z_stem=""
142	  local z_upper=""
143	  for z_name in \
144	    RBCC_moorings_dir    \
145	    RBCC_vessels_subdir  \
146	    RBCC_account_governor   \
147	    RBCC_account_retriever  \
148	    RBCC_account_director   \
149	    RBCC_account_payor      \
150	    RBCC_account_assay      \
151	    RBCC_account_mason      \
152	    RBCC_rbrr_file       \
153	    RBCC_rbrp_file       \
154	    RBCC_rbrm_file       \
155	    RBCC_rbrd_basename   \
156	    RBCC_rbrd_file       \
157	    RBCC_rbrs_file       \
158	    RBCC_rbrn_file       \
159	    RBCC_rbra_file       \
160	    RBCC_rbro_file       \
161	    RBCC_verb_divest     \
162	    RBCC_verb_invest     \
163	    RBCC_verb_roster     \
164	    RBCC_verb_enshrine   \
165	    RBCC_verb_inscribe   \
166	    RBCC_verb_kludge     \
167	    RBCC_verb_ordain     \
168	    RBCC_verb_yoke       \
169	    RBCC_container_bottle    \
170	    RBCC_container_pentacle  \
171	    RBCC_container_sentry    \
172	  ; do
173	    z_stem="${z_name#RBCC_}"
174	    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_stem}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]')"
175	    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}" \
176	      || buc_die "rbcc_emit_consts: emit failed for ${z_name}"
177	  done
178	}
179	
180	######################################################################
181	# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)
182	
183	zrbcc_kindle() {
184	  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"
185	
186	  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
187	  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10
188	  readonly RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC=60
189	
190	  readonly ZRBCC_KINDLED=1
191	}
192	
193	zrbcc_sentinel() {
194	  test "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbcc not kindled - call zrbcc_kindle first"
195	}

21	set -euo pipefail
22	
23	# Multiple inclusion detection
24	test -z "${ZRBA_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
25	ZRBA_SOURCED=1

27	######################################################################
28	# Internal Functions (zrba_*)
29	
30	zrba_kindle() {
31	  test -z "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba already kindled"
32	
33	  # Ensure dependency kindled first (rba mints tokens via rbgo)
34	  zrbgo_sentinel
35	
36	  readonly ZRBA_KINDLED=1
37	}
38	
39	zrba_sentinel() {
40	  test "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rba not kindled - call zrba_kindle first"
41	}
42	
43	######################################################################
44	# External / RBTOE Pattern Functions
45	
46	rba_get_governor_token_capture() {
47	  zrba_sentinel
48	
49	  # Need access to RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE from regime
50	  test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}" || return 1
51	
52	  local z_token
53	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}") || return 1
54	
55	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
56	  echo    "${z_token}"
57	}
58	
59	rba_authenticate_role_capture() {
60	  zrba_sentinel
61	
62	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
63	
64	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
65	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
66	
67	  buc_log_args "Authenticating with RBRA file: ${z_rbra_file}"
68	
69	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
70	
71	  local z_token
72	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || return 1
73	
74	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
75	
76	  echo "${z_token}"
77	}
78	
79	rba_extract_json_to_rbra() {
80	  zrba_sentinel
81	
82	  local -r z_json_path="$1"
83	  local -r z_rbra_path="$2"
84	  local -r z_lifetime_sec="$3"
85	  local -r z_expected_project_id="${4:-}"
86	
87	  test -f "${z_json_path}" || buc_die "Service account JSON not found: ${z_json_path}"
88	
89	  buc_info "Extracting service account credentials from JSON"
90	
91	  buc_log_args 'Extract fields'
92	  local z_client_email
93	  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_json_path}") \
94	                                        || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
95	  test -n "${z_client_email}"           || buc_die "Empty client_email in JSON"
96	  test    "${z_client_email}" != "null" || buc_die "Null client_email in JSON"
97	
98	  local z_private_key
99	  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_json_path}") \
100	                                       || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
101	  test -n "${z_private_key}"           || buc_die "Empty private_key in JSON"
102	  test    "${z_private_key}" != "null" || buc_die "Null private_key in JSON"
103	
104	  local z_project_id
105	  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_json_path}") \
106	                                      || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
107	  test -n "${z_project_id}"           || buc_die "Empty project_id in JSON"
108	  test    "${z_project_id}" != "null" || buc_die "Null project_id in JSON"
109	
110	  if test -n "${z_expected_project_id}"; then
111	    buc_log_args "Verify project matches expected: ${z_expected_project_id}"
112	    test "${z_project_id}" = "${z_expected_project_id}" \
113	      || buc_die "Project mismatch: JSON has '${z_project_id}', expected '${z_expected_project_id}'"
114	  else
115	    buc_log_args "No project validation - accepting JSON project_id: ${z_project_id}"
116	  fi
117	
118	  buc_log_args 'Write RBRA file'
119	  # CAUTION: jq -r unescapes JSON \n to real newlines, so z_private_key holds a
120	  # multi-line PEM string. printf '%s' below preserves real newlines into the
121	  # RBRA file. Consumer (rbgo_OAuth.sh:zrbgo_build_jwt_capture) tolerates either
122	  # real-newline or '\n'-escape form via printf '%b'; do not "normalize" to one
123	  # form without auditing the consumer.
124	  {
125	    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
126	    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
127	    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
128	    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=%s\n'  "${z_lifetime_sec}"
129	  } > "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_path}"
130	
131	  test -f "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file: ${z_rbra_path}"
132	
133	  buc_warn "Consider deleting source JSON after verification: ${z_json_path}"
134	}
135	
136	# RBTOE: RBRA Load Pattern
137	# Sources an RBRA file and validates required fields
138	rba_rbra_load() {
139	  zrba_sentinel
140	
141	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
142	
143	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file path required"
144	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file not found: ${z_rbra_file}"
145	
146	  buc_log_args "Loading and validating RBRA credentials from ${z_rbra_file}"
147	
148	  # Source the RBRA file
149	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: failed to source RBRA file"
150	
151	  # Validate required fields
152	  test -n "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
153	  test -n "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
154	  test -n "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
155	
156	  # Check for null values
157	  test "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
158	  test "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
159	  test "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
160	
161	  buc_log_args "RBRA validation successful: ${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL} in project ${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}"
162	}
163	
164	# RBTOE: RBRO Load Pattern

103	
104	  # Build request and pipe to curl - secrets never touch disk
105	  local z_response
106	  z_response=$(jq -n \
107	    --arg refresh_token "${RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN}" \
108	    --arg client_id "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID}" \
109	    --arg client_secret "${RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET}" \
110	    --arg grant_type "refresh_token" \
111	    '{
112	      refresh_token: $refresh_token,
113	      client_id: $client_id,
114	      client_secret: $client_secret,
115	      grant_type: $grant_type
116	    }' | curl -sS -X POST \
117	      --connect-timeout "${RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC}" \
118	      --max-time "${RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC}" \
119	      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
120	      -d @- \
121	      "${RBGC_OAUTH_TOKEN_URL}") || buc_die "Failed to execute OAuth refresh request"
122

124	  local z_error
125	  z_error=$(jq -r '.error // empty' <<<"${z_response}")
126	  if test -n "${z_error}"; then
127	    local z_error_desc
128	    z_error_desc=$(jq -r '.error_description // .error // "Unknown error"' <<<"${z_response}")

142	# and freshness; refresh tokens are long-lived so the extra roundtrips are cheap
143	# relative to the depot operations they authorize, and uncached tokens can't grow
144	# stale between distinct Payor ceremonies.

154	  test -n "${RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID:-}" || buc_die "RBRP_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set in environment"

156	  # Exchange refresh token for access token
157	  local z_access_token
158	  z_access_token=$(zrbgp_refresh_capture) || buc_die "Failed to exchange OAuth refresh token"
159	  
160	  test -n "${z_access_token}" || buc_die "Empty access token from OAuth exchange"

179	#

187	#

189	zrbgp_depot_state_emit() {

191

197	
198	  # Anchor prefix string used inside loop for displayName stripping.
199	  local -r z_display_prefix="${RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX} "

1	# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-27
4	
5	Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
6	per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
7	organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
8	the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
9	single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
10	roadmap entry.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## The plan
15	
16	RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:
17	
18	- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
19	  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
20	- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
21	  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.
22	
23	A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
24	exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
25	codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
26	requirement, which is also the natural paywall.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## Two models, two tiers
31	
32	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
33	|---|---|---|
34	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
35	| GCP organization | not required | required |
36	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
37	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
38	| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |
39	
40	Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
41	Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
42	model-blind.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## The organization requirement is the tier boundary
47	
48	Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
49	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
50	structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.
51	
52	An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
53	creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
54	tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
55	pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
56	verify a domain.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products
61	
62	GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
63	authenticating?*
64	
65	| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
66	|---|---|---|
67	| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
68	| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
69	| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |
70	
71	RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
72	actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## How federation authenticates
77	
78	The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
79	touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
80	**never runtime**.
81	
82	**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
83	workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
84	issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
85	claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
86	receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
87	`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
88	Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
89	already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
90	roster.
91	
92	**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
93	IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
94	(RFC 8628):
95	
96	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
97	   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
98	2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
99	   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
100	   is not involved.
101	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
102	
103	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
104	
105	```
106	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
107	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
108	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
109	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
110	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
111	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
112	```
113	
114	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
115	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
116	
117	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
118	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
119	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
120	long session.
121	
122	---
123	
124	## IdP landscape
125	
126	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
127	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
128	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
129	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
130	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
131	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
132	
133	---
134	
135	## Refresh-token policy
136	
137	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
138	Confine it to exactly one place:
139	
140	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
141	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
142	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
143	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
144	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
145	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
146	  token.
147	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
148	  outside the operator mode enum.
149	
150	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
151	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
152	
153	---
154	
155	## Where configuration lives
156	
157	Three homes, by scope:
158	
159	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
160	  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
161	  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
162	  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
163	  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
164	  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
165	  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
166	  independently straddle modes.
167	- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
168	  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
169	  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
170	- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
171	  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.
172	
173	There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
174	the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
175	to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
176	secret.
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Operator-admin verbs
181	
182	The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
183	**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
184	operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
185	keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
186	on the operator's `principal://` subject.
187	
188	Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
189	**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
190	trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
191	enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.
192	
193	---
194	
195	## One consumer path, real and synthetic
196	
197	Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
198	resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
199	only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
200	device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
201	token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
202	path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## The single code seam
207	
208	Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
209	token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —
210	
211	- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
212	- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.
213	
214	Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
215	adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.
216	
217	Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
218	pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
219	enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
220	call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
221	second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
222	again.
223	
224	**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
225	the federation branch.**
226	
227	---
228	
229	## Open at implementation time
230	
231	- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
232	- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
233	  → what the token resolver consumes).
234	- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
235	  calls.
236	
237	---
238	
239	## Sources
240	
241	- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
242	- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
243	- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
244	- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
245	- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
246	- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)

1	# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)
2	
3	*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
4	crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
5	repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
6	canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*
7	
8	## Headline
9	
10	The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
11	an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
12	lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
13	The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
14	separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
15	hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.
16	
17	## Objects
18	
19	- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
20	  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
21	- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
22	  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
23	- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
24	  a **Resource**.
25	- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
26	  repo**, the **Mason SA**.
27	
28	## Three independent lifecycles
29	
30	1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
31	   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
32	2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
33	   credential; the SA is untouched.*
34	3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
35	   by member = SA.
36	
37	Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
38	kill the old one.
39	
40	## The hole
41	
42	The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
43	**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
44	does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:
45	
46	- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
47	  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
48	  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
49	  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
50	- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
51	  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
52	  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
53	  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.
54	
55	## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)
56	
57	- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
58	  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
59	  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
60	  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
61	  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
62	  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
63	  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
64	- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
65	  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
66	  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
67	  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
68	  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
69	  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
70	  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
71	  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
72	- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
73	  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
74	  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
75	  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
76	  a not-yet-converged view.
77	
78	Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
79	routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
80	crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.
81	
82	## The fix — separate the lifecycles
83	
84	Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:
85	
86	- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
87	  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
88	  already established.
89	- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
90	  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
91	  Bindings.
92	- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
93	  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
94	  rather than every cycle.
95	
96	This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
97	and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:
98	
99	1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
100	   teardown).
101	2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
102	3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
103	4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
104	5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
105	   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
106	   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
107	6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
108	   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
109	   shows the policy is writable).
110	7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
111	   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
112	8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
113	   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.
114	
115	## Footnote: federation
116	
117	The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
118	grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
119	no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
120	**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
121	repaired in place as above.
122	
123	## Evidence appendix
124	
125	- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
126	  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
127	  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
128	- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
129	  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
130	- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
131	  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
132	- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
133	- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
134	  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
135	- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
136	  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
137	  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
138	  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).
139	
140	[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
141	[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies
142	
143	## Addendum (2026-06-05): wiring the revoke half exposed a symmetric Class-C race
144	
145	H1's repair (revoke-before-delete) added resource-scope `getIamPolicy` /
146	`setIamPolicy` calls on the **teardown** path. The first cut leaned them clean of
147	propagation tolerance, reasoning a revoke needs no member-visibility wait. That
148	conflated two independent things: the member-visibility classes (A "does not
149	exist" / B "is not deleted"), which are about the *member* being added or removed
150	and genuinely don't gate a revoke read, and **Class C**, which is about the

1	# Governor mantle tombstone leak — H1's scoped-out cousin (keyfile tier)
2	
3	*2026-06-05. Surfaced while live-validating rbk-08-credential-repairs' H1/H2 fix
4	against the canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`. Captured as input
5	to rbk-14-mvp-prepare-federation-idea, whose generic-teardown convergence would
6	subsume the fix — so this is a concept capture, not a call to build now.*
7	
8	## Observation
9	
10	`gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor token, 2026-06-05)
11	shows **8** `deleted:serviceAccount:governor-<datestamp>@…?uid=…` tombstones, and the
12	count rose **7→8** across a single `canonical-invest` run — one fresh ghost per
13	governor re-mantle. The grounding memo recorded ~30 governor-owner ghosts pre-H1-fix;
14	the leak has run unbounded since.
15	
16	## Mechanism — H1, one tier up
17	
18	`rbgp_governor_mantle` (`Tools/rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh`, "Clean up existing governor-*
19	service accounts") lists every `governor-*` SA and **DELETEs each one before minting a
20	new datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHMM`** — with **no revoke of its bindings first**.
21	The outgoing governor holds `roles/owner` (plus its establish grants) on the depot
22	project; deleting a still-bound member is exactly the case GCP cannot drop — it
23	retags the binding `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps it. Identical to H1 (director/retriever
24	divest deleting before revoking), on the governor/mantle path. The caller is the
25	**Payor** (project owner), so the leak is purely the missing revoke, not a permission
26	gap.
27	
28	This was deliberately out of scope for rbk-08: the grounding memo's point 8 cinched
29	"Governor out of scope (deliberate single-governor, datestamped, low-churn)." Correct
30	call for that pace — but the hole is real and unbounded, marching toward GCP's
31	~1500-member allow-policy cap like its siblings.
32	
33	## Three ways it closes — in increasing order of remodel
34	
35	1. **Targeted fix (no remodel).** Wire revoke-before-delete into the mantle delete
36	   loop: for each outgoing `governor-*`, `rbgi_revoke_project_member` its known grants
37	   (`roles/owner` + the establish set) before the DELETE. All project-scope, caller is
38	   the Payor who already owns the project → **lean** (no Class-C tolerance needed —
39	   simpler than the director case, whose repo/SA resource-scope revokes needed it).
40	   Smallest possible change; reuses the rbk-08 revoke layer verbatim.
41	
42	2. **Subsumed by rbk-14 (this heat).** rbk-14 converges the admin verbs so teardown is
43	   "generic across roles" and `mantle` becomes `remove`+`add` parameterized by
44	   capability. Two sub-outcomes, both fix the leak:
45	   - if the governor stays delete+recreate, the generic revoke-before-delete covers it
46	     like any role;
47	   - better, if the convergence makes the governor **idempotent** (establish-once +
48	     rekey, as H2 did for director/retriever), the delete disappears entirely — no SA
49	     churn, no datestamp, no tombstone.
50	   Either way a separately-built targeted fix (1) becomes **wasted work** once rbk-14
51	   lands.
52	
53	3. **Obviated by federation (beyond rbk-14).** The federation tier (RBSHR;
54	   `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`) has no per-cycle SA at all — stable
55	   `principal://` subjects with central revocation — so there is no governor SA to
56	   tombstone.
57	
58	## Caveat for rbk-14 implementers — equivalent problems
59	
60	The convergence's generic governor teardown inherits rbk-08's propagation lessons and
61	should **reuse, not reinvent**:
62	
63	- revoke-before-delete on the governor needs the `rbgi_revoke_*` layer (built, proven);
64	- any ordering where a freshly-minted governor revokes the outgoing one's bindings is a
65	  **Class-C** (caller-recently-empowered) site against whatever scope it touches — the
66	  exact race rbk-08 mapped (RBSCIP) and tolerated;
67	- a delete-then-recreate governor would face the SA delete read-path flap
68	  (`RBGC_GONE_CONFIRM_STREAK`); datestamped names currently sidestep it, idempotent
69	  rekey eliminates it.
70	
71	## Recommendation
72	
73	Do **not** build the targeted fix now — it is most likely subsumed by rbk-14. This
74	memo is the capture so the concept is not lost; rbk-14's paddock references it as the
75	fallback for the no-convergence (or governor-descoped) branch, and as the flag that
76	governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius and must reuse the rbk-08
77	propagation machinery.
78	
79	## Sources
80	
81	- Code: `rbgp_governor_mantle` (`Tools/rbk/rbgp_Payor.sh`, governor cleanup loop);
82	  `rbgi_revoke_project_member` (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`).
83	- `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md` (H1/H2 + the
84	  point-8 governor scope-out; revoke-side Class-C addendum).
85	- RBSCIP propagation classes (Class C, caller-recently-empowered).
86	- rbk-14-mvp-prepare-federation-idea paddock: generic-teardown convergence.
87

content.content:
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)

content.text:
The paddock is tracked in git but not as a file I can directly access. Based on the user's statement that the paddock is "undefined," it appears to be a work in progress. Now let me synthesize my findings:

## Key Findings:

1. **Governor mantle revoke-before-delete is NOT implemented** (lines 204-206 of memo claim it should be, but code line 1748 deletes without revoking)

2. **The scoping question about role-keyed vs identity-keyed accessor is LEFT OPEN** (memo lines 237-238, paddock is undefined)

3. **Account naming follows `director-<identity>` pattern** (per spec RBSDK line 12 and code line 658/607) - this is role-keyed today

4. **RBRA file paths are consumed at ~39 call sites** (memo says ~30, actual count is 39)

5. **The memo claims divergence from 20260527** (lines 230-231: "one SA *per person*, not per role") - but the current code implements per-role SAs

6. **Orphan marker uses description sentinel, not SA tags** (verified per memo line 143-144, code doesn't yet implement the marker)

7. **Identity verb decoupling (establish + rekey + remove) is NOT yet implemented** - the code still uses the fused model per memo-20260604

Let me now prepare my structured findings:

findings.title: Governor mantle revoke-before-delete gap
findings.issue: The governor_mantle function deletes existing governor-* service accounts without first revoking their roles/owner binding. This creates deleted:…?uid= tombstones on the project policy, accumulating without bound across re-mantles. The memo (lines 204-206) explicitly specifies this should be fixed with 'the generic revoke-before-delete', reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer, but the code contains no revoke call in this path.
findings.evidence: Code line 1748 (rbuh_json DELETE) deletes each governor-* SA directly. Memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md lines 204-206: 'The leak closes cheaply: add the generic revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change.' See also memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md documenting 8 observed tombstones on the canonical depot.
findings.suggested_fix: Before the DELETE at rbgp_Payor.sh:1748, call rbgi_revoke_project_member for roles/owner (and any other established grants) on the outgoing governor SA. This is project-scoped and caller (Payor) is the project owner, so no Class-C retry tolerance is needed (unlike resource-scope revokes on director/retriever).

findings.title: Scoping ambiguity: role-keyed vs identity-keyed accessor is unresolved
findings.issue: The memo (lines 237-238) explicitly states an open scoping question: 'Whether the heat's load-bearing core is the role-keyed no-behavior stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.' The paddock is undefined (per user statement). Without this resolution, the design's intent on whether this heat delivers one-SA-per-person (memo line 187, the stated goal) or role-keyed status-quo (the implied current state per code inspection) is unclear. This is load-bearing: it affects the token accessor architecture, the RBRA file path structure, and the migration cost.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 187-191 define 'One citizen per person = one SA' as the end-state goal. Memo lines 233-238 describe the divergence: '20260527's single code seam is a role-keyed token accessor… Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes identity-keyed… (Whether the heat's load-bearing core is the role-keyed no-behavior stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)' Current code (rbgg_Governor.sh line 658) implements director-<identity>, which is role-keyed (one SA per role per identity).
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify in the paddock whether this heat's MVP is (a) role-keyed, no-behavior-change (status quo with capability decoupling), or (b) identity-keyed, one-SA-per-person (the long-term goal). If (a), update the memo's 'This heat supersedes both' claim (line 230-231) to clarify it's incremental, not a full supersession. If (b), document the migration hazard and token accessor refactoring scope. Anchor the clarification in the paddock's stated scope section.

findings.title: Memo claims identity verb decoupling (establish/rekey/remove) but code is still fused
findings.issue: Memo-20260604 (the H1/H2 fix spec, which memo-20260605 references as an enabler at line 206) specifies decomposing the fused invest into: (1) Establish = ensure SA exists and grant bindings once, idempotent; (2) Re-key = rotate key only, presumes established; (3) Remove = revoke all bindings, then delete SA. However, the code still implements the old fused model: zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key (lines 159-364) does SA-create + binding-grant + key-mint all together, and divest has no revoke. The memo (line 206) claims this is an 'enabler', but if the split is not implemented, the governor tombstone fix is incomplete.
findings.evidence: Memo-20260604 lines 82-94: 'Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations: Establish (one-time per SA… grant its Bindings once. Idempotent…), Re-key (every issuance: rotate the Key only), Teardown (rare… revoke Bindings, then delete the SA).' Code rbgg_Governor.sh:zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key still executes SA-create (if absent) + binding-grant + key-mint atomically. Memo-20260605 line 206 states the mantle fix 'reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer' which is the H1 fix, implying H1 decomposition should be in place, but the code shows no establish/rekey/remove boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify the dependency: Is the governor revoke-before-delete fix (finding 1) a standalone targeted fix requiring no decomposition, OR is it contingent on the H1 establish/rekey/remove refactoring? If standalone, document the fix in rbgp_governor_mantle as a narrowly-scoped revoke-then-delete. If contingent, update the paddock to scope the establish/rekey/remove decomposition as a prerequisite, and document the implementation phase.

findings.title: Memo claims 'one SA per person' but code implements 'director/retriever per role per identity'
findings.issue: Memo lines 230-231 state 'the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.' However, the spec RBSDK line 12 documents the current design as 'the SA account name is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`' where identity is operator-supplied, and the code (rbgg_Governor.sh line 658) implements exactly that. This means there is one director SA per (identity), one retriever SA per (identity), etc. — still role-keyed, not person-keyed. The memo's claim at line 230-231 is either aspirational (future end-state) or incorrectly asserts the current state as the new design.
findings.evidence: Memo line 230-231: 'This heat supersedes both: the verb bodies converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.' RBSDK line 12: 'the SA account name is composed as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`'. Code rbgg_Governor.sh line 658: `local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"`. This creates director-alice, director-bob, retriever-alice, retriever-bob — four SAs for two people, organized by role.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) If this heat DOES deliver one-SA-per-person, update the code and specs to use identity-only SA names (e.g., director-alice holds both director and retriever roles), and update the investment/divest paths to grant multiple capability-sets on one SA. (b) If this heat DOES NOT deliver one-SA-per-person, correct the memo to clarify that the 'supersession' is on the capability decoupling layer only (grant/revoke/roster verbatim across tiers), not on the identity layer (which remains role-keyed in this heat). Add a forward-reference to a future heat for the per-person migration.

findings.title: Orphan marker (SA description sentinel) not yet implemented in code
findings.issue: The memo specifies at line 143-144 that SA identity markers (to catch orphaned failed-add SAs) should use 'SA tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a label.' The code creates SAs with displayName and description at rbgg_Governor.sh lines 198-199, but does not set any `rbk:…` description sentinel value to mark RBK-managed SAs for orphan sweeps. Without this, a failed add-citizen (SA created, but credential delivery fails) would create an untrackable SA.
findings.evidence: Memo lines 142-144: 'Marker options: SA tags (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change). SA `description` sentinel (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.' Code rbgg_Governor.sh:197-199 sets displayName but no description sentinel (description is set to operational text only, not an `rbk:` marker).
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `rbk:` sentinel to the description field when creating director/retriever/governor SAs (e.g., prepend 'rbk:managed ' or include 'rbk=<identity>' in the description). Document the sentinel pattern and implement an orphan-sweep verb that lists all SAs matching the sentinel in the description. This is citizen-tier-specific and a separate concern from the hot-path credential grant/revoke.

findings.title: Spec RBSDK claims identity-keyed composition, but code shows role-keyed
findings.issue: RBSDK line 30 documents the SA composition as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`, which is a role-keyed pattern (the role name 'director' is hardcoded). The operation is parameterized by identity (param1), not by both role and identity. This is consistent with the code but conflicts with the memo's claim that this heat delivers 'one SA per person' (which would require dropping the role name from the SA ID).
findings.evidence: RBSDK line 30: '`{{rbbc_store}} {{rbbc_account_name}} as `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>` (operator-supplied identity composing the account name)'. Code rbgg_Governor.sh:658: `local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"`. Both hard-code the role (director).
findings.suggested_fix: No fix needed if this is intentional role-keyed design for this heat. Instead, clarify the memo (line 230-231) to state 'one SA per [role, identity] pair' to be precise. If moving to one-SA-per-person is a future heat, document that transition as a separate effort and link from the paddock.
